coredata.dc:title: High performance hybrid supercapacitor based on doped zucchini-derived carbon dots and graphene
coredata.dc:creator: Hoang, Van Chinh
Gomes, Vincent G.
coredata.dc:description: Zucchini-derived carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a green and low-cost synthetic route using waste zucchini biomass as an inexpensive and naturally available carbon precursor. Apart from a cost neutral resource, the rationale for this choice are the porous nature and the presence of natural carbon and oxygen in the precursor. The as-prepared CDs were hydrothermally treated with graphene oxide by a two-step procedure: hydrothermal process to assemble three dimensionally porous RGO/CDs composites, followed by facile reduction by HI vapor, where CDs serve as spacers to hinder restacking of graphene nanosheets and HI treatment deoxygenates the composites to enhance electrical conductivity and dope heteroatoms in graphene. The electrochemical performance of the composites with variable mass loadings of CDs (0–50 wt.%) and reduction time (0–30 min) was investigated. For the optimal CD mass loading of 33 wt.% at 10 min reduction time, the resulting graphene/CD composite had 6.1 wt.% I, 185 m2 g−1 surface area and 0.58 cc g−1 total pore volume. The composite specific capacitance was 374 F g−1 at 2 mV s−1 with excellent capacitance retention of 71.7% as the scan rate increased from 2 to 1000 mV s−1. The composite exhibited superior cycling stability over 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 with 93.8% capacitance retention. The composite results based on the physicochemical properties, and the ionic conductivity and diffusivity demonstrate the development of a sustainable supercapacitor product from agricultural wastes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Graphene
Agricultural waste
Iodine doping
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images and (b) FTIR spectra of CDs.
SEM images of (a) RGO, (b) RC25, (c) RC33 and (d) RC50. The scale bar is 1 μm.

(a) CV curves at 50 mV s−1; (b) galvanostatic charge/discharge curves at 5 A g−1 of RGO and RCx composites (x = 25, 33 and 50); (c) specific capacitances calculated from CV, (d) specific capacitances from GCD curves.
SEM images of RC33 after (a) 0 min, (b) 5 min, (c) 10 min, (d) 15 min and (e) 30 min HI treatment. The scale bar is 1 μm; (f) TEM image of RC33 after 10 min HI treatment.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms; (b) pore size distributions of RC33 treated with HI vapor for various reduction times.
(a) Raman and (b) and FTIR spectra of RC33 treated with HI vapor for various reduction times.
XPS C1s spectra of RC33 treated with HI vapor for: (a) 10 min and (b) 30 min. High resolution I 3d5/2 spectra of RC33 following HI treatment after: (c) 10 min and (d) 30 min.
(a) CV curves at 50 mV s−1 and (b) GCD curves at 5 A g−1 of RC33 treated with HI vapor for various reduction times; specific capacitance of materials calculated from its CV (c) and GCD curves (d).

(a) Cycling stability of HI-RC33-10 at 10 A g−1 over 10,000 galvanostatic charge/discharge (inset shows the last ten charge/discharge curves for 10,000 cycles); (b) CV curves of HI-RC33-10 before and after 10,000 GCD cycles.
(a) Nyquist plots and (b) the relationship between Z′ and ω−1/2 of RC33 treated with HI vapor for various reduction times.
(a) Gravimetric capacitance versus inverse square root of scan rate of RC33 treated with HI vapor for various reduction times, (b) Dependence of surface/bulk charge ratio on scan rate of HI-RC33-10.
Proposed reduction mechanism by HI treatment.
Elemental composition and BET surface area of samples.
Elemental composition and BET surface area of RC33 treated with HI vapor as a function of time.
Warburg prefactor and diffusion coefficient of OH− (DOH-) of RC33 treated with HI vapor as a function of time.

head.ce:title: High performance hybrid supercapacitor based on doped zucchini-derived carbon dots and graphene

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, NSW 2006, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zucchini-derived carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a green and low-cost synthetic route using waste zucchini biomass as an inexpensive and naturally available carbon precursor. Apart from a cost neutral resource, the rationale for this choice are the porous nature and the presence of natural carbon and oxygen in the precursor. The as-prepared CDs were hydrothermally treated with graphene oxide by a two-step procedure: hydrothermal process to assemble three dimensionally porous RGO/CDs composites, followed by facile reduction by HI vapor, where CDs serve as spacers to hinder restacking of graphene nanosheets and HI treatment deoxygenates the composites to enhance electrical conductivity and dope heteroatoms in graphene. The electrochemical performance of the composites with variable mass loadings of CDs (0–50 wt.%) and reduction time (0–30 min) was investigated. For the optimal CD mass loading of 33 wt.% at 10 min reduction time, the resulting graphene/CD composite had 6.1 wt.% I, 185 m2 g−1 surface area and 0.58 cc g−1 total pore volume. The composite specific capacitance was 374 F g−1 at 2 mV s−1 with excellent capacitance retention of 71.7% as the scan rate increased from 2 to 1000 mV s−1. The composite exhibited superior cycling stability over 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 with 93.8% capacitance retention. The composite results based on the physicochemical properties, and the ionic conductivity and diffusivity demonstrate the development of a sustainable supercapacitor product from agricultural wastes.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical capacitors have attracted remarkable attention because of their rapid charge/discharge rate, high power density and long cycle life capability [1,2], yet their low energy density compared to the commercially available lithium ion batteries and fuel cells makes them unsatisfactory for the increasing demands on the burgeoning markets of portable electronic devices and electric vehicles. These undesirable attributes greatly inhibit their wider application in energy storage systems [3,4]. Therefore, it would be necessary to boost their energy density at levels close to or even surpass that of the current batteries without sacrificing their positive electrochemical features.

Supercapacitors store electricity in two ways: electrostatic attraction of charges at the interface between the electrolyte and the electrode (electrical double layer capacitance, EDLC) and reversible surface or near-surface oxidation/reduction (pseudo-capacitance) [5]. Previous research has shown that the performance of supercapacitors depends on the characteristics of electrode materials [6,7]. Usually materials with high conductivity and large surface area facilitate fast charge transfer and rapid ion transport, leading to excellent EDLC and rate capability [8]. Although transition metal oxides and conducting polymers are able to provide high pseudocapacitance, they suffer from cycling instability and rate capability originating from their poor electrical conductivity and structural swelling/shrinkage during charge/discharge processes [3,8,9].

Carbon materials are widely employed as electrode materials due to their abundance, high electrical conductivity, large surface area and chemical inertness [8,9]. Among them, graphene with its exceptional characteristics, such as, superior surface area (2630 m2 g−1) and extraordinary electrical conductivity has been extensively studied for charge storage [10], yet its practically achievable specific capacitance is far below the theoretically predicted value (550 Fg−1). This is largely due to the dense and compact network within graphene caused by aggregation/stacking of graphene sheets, hindering electrolyte accessibility at high charge/discharge rates [3,9,11]. To overcome this problem, several approaches have been proposed, for example, the assembly of 3D hierarchical porous structure [12,13], incorporation of graphene with pseudocapacitive materials such as transition metal oxides [14,15] or conducting polymers [16–18]. Nevertheless, the addition of these materials seriously compromises both cyclability and rate capability of graphene [3,19]. Recently, coupling graphene with another allotrope of carbon, such as carbon nanotube [9] or carbon nanofiber [20] has presented efficient pathways for enhanced electrochemical performance, yet the high cost of these additives inhibit commercialization of the resulting composites.

Carbon quantum dot (CD) has emerged as an important class of 0D carbon nanomaterial for applications ranging from optoelectronics to biomedical systems due to their chemical stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility and tunable photoluminescence [21,22]. Recently, several reports have documented that CDs could enhance the supercapacitance of graphene. For example, reduced graphene oxide (RGO) anchored with CDs (RGO:CDs = 1:1 w:w) delivered a capacitance of 211.9 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, compared to 121.6 F g−1 of RGO [23]. Similarly, Zhao et al. [11] synthesized an interconnected 3D CDs/RGO, which displayed a maximum specific capacitance of 308 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 and superior cycle life with 92% capacitance retention over 20,000 cycles at 10 A g−1. In another case, 3D porous RGO hydrogel/CDs exhibits high specific capacitances of 264 and 210 F g−1 at current densities of 1 and 10 A g−1, respectively with good cycle life (9.1% deterioration over 5000 cycles), together with a promising energy density of 35.3 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 516 W kg−1 [24].

Recently, micro-supercapacitors based on CDs/graphene fibers showed a much improvement in energy storage capability with larger capacitance of 607 mF cm−2 and high energy density of 67.37 μW h cm−2 [25]. The enhanced capacitance could be attributed to the effective prevention of graphene restacking as well as the pseudocapacitance contribution from oxygen containing groups on the surface of carbon dots [11,25,26]. However, the obtained capacitance values are modest compared to the theoretical potential of graphene. This may be due to the high concentration of oxygen on both RGO and CDs, and this may contribute towards low electrical conductivity [11]. Several methods have been employed to deoxygenate graphene oxide (GO) to obtain high quality graphene including thermal and chemical reduction [27]. The former involved thermal annealing at high temperatures (above 800 °C) and photo-irradiation that are generally time-consuming and inefficient; while chemical reduction is advantageous for mass production [27].

Chemical reductions often use reducing agents such as hydrazine [28] or NaBH4 [29] yet both are suitable for GO reduction in solution phase only and result in low electrical conductivity of RGO, impeding their use in the reduction of solid materials [30]. Recent studies demonstrated the advantage of using HI as a facile means to efficiently convert GO membranes into high quality RGO films within a short time (2–60 min) and at low temperature (≤100 °C) [30–34]. It was found that the conductivity of GO significantly increased after HI treatment, which resulted from higher graphitization of π conjugated graphene network, fewer structural defects and iodine doping [30,32]. The doping of I heteroatoms into graphene nanostructure might also introduce some pseudocapacitance to the electrode material for an enhanced electrochemical performance.

Another obstacle is in the use of carbonaceous precursors for the synthesis of CDs. Typically, CDs are fabricated from conventional chemicals, such as, citric acid [3,11,23], glucose [24], heparin sodium and protamine sulfate [26]. The key challenge for the utilization of CDs in practical applications is to mass produce sustainable CDs via cost-effective strategies. In an alternative approach, there have been reports on inexpensive and green fabrication of carbon dots from natural precursors such as orange juice [35], cauliflower leaves [36], pakchoi [37], soy milk [38], cabbage [39], pomelo peel [40] and egg [41]. Zucchini is widely grown globally at a low-price with rapid growth. During its production, large quantities of zucchini with poor appearance and small size are discarded as wastes. Therefore, the utilization of waste zucchini biomass for value-added applications is highly desirable. Herein, we present a facile procedure of preparing environmentally-friendly carbon quantum dots from imperfect zucchini as a biomass source, and fabricate these CDs with reduced graphene oxide via a two-step procedure: hydrothermal process to assemble 3D porous RGO/CDs composites, followed by deoxygenation by HI vapor to simultaneously increase their electrical conductivity and dope I heteroatoms into the structure. The effect of CDs and reducing time of HI treatment on the electrochemical performance of the materials are presented in terms of their physicochemical properties and ionic (OH−) diffusivity.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Australia) and used as received without purification. Milli-Q (MQ) water (18.2 MΩ cm resistivity) was used throughout our experiments unless otherwise stated. Imperfect zucchini which was collected from a local supermarket were washed by MQ water and then chopped into small pieces. Subsequently, they were added together with MQ water into a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 220 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the product was collected by filtration through 0.45 μm nitrocellulose membrane, followed by dialysis (MWCO ∼10 kDa) for 24 h. Finally, the solution was concentrated to 6.2 mg mL−1 and stored in a fridge for further purposes.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by modified Hummer's method [36]. The RGO/CD composites were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, variable amounts of CDs were added into 10 mL GO solution 2 mg mL−1, followed by ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. The resulting suspension was then transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-sealed stainless steel autoclave, and kept at 200 °C for 10 h. The composite was separated by filtration, washed several times by MQ water before freeze drying at −40 °C overnight. To investigate the effect of CDs on the electrochemical performance of the materials, the mass loading of CDs was controlled to be 0, 25, 33 and 50 wt.%. The obtained composite was labeled as RCx, where R and C refer to RGO and CDs, respectively, and x represents the mass loadings of CDs (x = 0, 25, 33 and 50). The RC33 was further treated with 57% HI acid vapor at 100 °C for 0–30 min, which was denoted as HI-RC33-y (y = 5, 10, 15 and 30). Subsequently, the products were filtered, washed several times by MQ water and dried at 70 °C overnight. For comparison, RGO was also treated by 57% HI acid vapor at 100 °C for 10 min, labeled as HI-RGO-10.

The morphology of as-prepared materials was investigated by scanning electronic microscope (SEM, Ultra-Plus, Zeiss) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-1400F, JEOL). High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was performed on a JEM 3000F (JEOL) at 300 kV. The element presence in the materials was characterized by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) in SEM at 10 kV. The Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) surface areas of the samples were examined by at 77 K (Quanta Chrome, autosorb-1) while the pore surface volume and pore size distribution were calculated adopting the density functional theory (DFT) method. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of samples were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. Raman spectra were performed on a Raman microscope (inVia, Renishaw) with a laser excitation at 532 nm. The spectra from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained using the ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Scientific, UK) with monochromated Al Kα source (energy 1486.61 eV) at 12.0 kV. UV–vis absorption was investigated using a Cary 60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were collected by a Horiba FluoroMax-4 (USA) spectrofluorometer.

The electrochemical measurements were performed on a three-electrode cell configuration in 1 M KOH electrolyte with glassy carbon (GC, 5 mm in diameter) loaded with RCx or HI-RC33-y as the working electrode, Pt plate as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The working electrodes were fabricated by ultra-sonicating the active materials and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) at a ratio of 90:10 in isopropanol. Afterwards, the resulting suspension was drop-casted on GC electrode and dried at room temperature.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge discharge (GCD) curves were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, CHI Instrument) in a potential window between −1 and 0 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded by applying an AC voltage of 5 mV perturbation amplitude in 106 0.01 Hz range. The specific capacitance of the composite was calculated by:
(1) Csp = Celectrode/m
where Celectrode is the measured capacitance and m is the mass of the active materials.

Celectrode was calculated from the following formulas:
(2)
C
e l e c t r o d e
=
1
2 V ν
∫
V
−
V
+
i
(
V
)
d V
where, i(V) is the current of CV scans, ν is the scan rate and V (V = V+ − V−) presents the scanned voltage window.
(3) Celectrode = i/(dV/dt)
where, i and dV/dt are the current and slope of the discharge plots, respectively.

Zucchini with an interconnected three dimensionally macroporous structure (Fig. S1, Supporting Information) contains abundance of C and O moieties (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The precursor was hydrothermally treated to yield the CDs. TEM results reveal that the as-prepared CDs has a spherical morphology with a size distribution between 3 and 10 nm and a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.209 nm, which corresponds to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1 a).

Fig. 1b shows the FTIR spectrum of the CDs, which presents several functional groups on their surface. For example, a broad band at 3000–3670 cm−1, clear modes at 1767 cm−1 and 1075 cm−1 were attributed to O H, CO and C–O stretching vibration, respectively [9]. The C–H stretch band (2935 cm−1) originates from aliphatic hydrocarbons of the zucchini while the peaks at 1389 cm−1 was assigned to C–H deformation of CH2 and CH3 groups [36]. The mode at 1291 cm−1 derives from C–OH vibration whereas the peaks at 1670 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1 are ascribed to CC stretching [39]. These hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups enables CDs to be highly dispersed in H2O. This is consistent with the EDX analysis, which indicates high C and O contents with trace amounts of other elements (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). Moreover, the optical properties of the CDs examined by UV–vis and PL spectroscopy (Figs. S4–6, Supporting Information) further confirm the successful formation of CDs.

Next, RGO/CDs composites were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal reaction [26]. The size of graphene hydrogel was found to be remarkably influenced by the addition of CDs (Fig. S7, Supporting Information), which might be assigned to the effective reduction in the π–π stacking of graphene sheets, beneficial for easy electrolyte accessibility for enhanced electrochemical performances [9,11]. The morphology and elemental composition of RGO and RCx samples were investigated by SEM and EDS, respectively. Fig. 2 a indicates that RGO possesses a typical 3D network with relatively packed RGO layers [12]. After incorporating with CDs, the RCx composites show more porous structure with larger pore sizes up to several μm (Fig. 2b–d). The addition of CDs into graphene network results in a rise in the weight percentage of oxygen and a decrease in the carbon concentration (see Table 1 and Figs. S8–11, Supporting Information). The oxygen functional groups improves the surface wettability of pure graphene and contributes to increased active redox sites which induces higher pseudocapacitance, leading to an enhanced electrochemical performance [9,11,26].

The electrochemical performances of the synthesized composites were first evaluated by CV, GCD and EIS in 1 M KOH electrolyte using a three-electrode cell configuration, and the results are demonstrated in Fig. 3 and Figs. S14–17 (Supporting Information). Fig. 3a exhibits reversible redox humps between −0.8 and −0.4 V, which are ascribed to the pseudo-capacitance of the oxygen functional groups [9,11]. GCD curves of all materials reveal triangular and nearly symmetrical shapes, suggesting a nearly ideal supercapacitor characteristic (Fig. 3b). The specific capacitances calculated from their CV and GCD curves are summarized in Fig. 3c and d. Among the samples with varied compositions, the RC33 exhibited the highest specific capacitance of 218 F g−1 at 2 mV s−1 compared to those of RGO (144 F g−1), RC25 (167 F g−1) and RC50 (195 F g−1). Notably, with an increase in the scan rate to 1000 mV s−1, RGO/CDs composites retain 61–62% of their capacitances whereas the capacitance retention of RGO drops to about 47%. The specific capacitance of the RC33 is comparable to recently reported RGO/CDs composites (see Table S1, Supporting Information). Insignificant differences were observed in the Raman and FTIR spectra among these materials (Fig. S12, Supporting Information). The improved electrochemical performance of RCx composites compared to RGO could be assigned to two major factors. First, abundant oxygen functional groups on the CDs increase active redox sites which induces higher pseudocapacitance (see Fig. S18a and Table S2, Supporting Information) [9,11,26]. Second, CDs serve as nanofillers to effectively hinder graphene restacking, which results in enhanced surface area (Table 1 and Fig. S13, Supporting Information), reduced charge transfer resistance (Fig. S16, Supporting Information) and increased ionic migration from the electrolyte to the surface of the materials (see Fig. S18b and Table S3, Supporting Information) [11,23–26,36,42].

We further enhanced the electrochemical performance of RC33 composite by a facile reduction in HI vapor. Fig. 4 a–e illustrate typical SEM micrographs of the RC33 after 0–30 min HI vapor reduction. With an increase in the reduction time, the 3D structure of RC33 became more densely packed with reduced pore sizes, indicating that RGO/CDs sheets stacked and aggregated due to the removal of oxygen containing groups. Especially, these pores almost disappeared after 30 min of reduction. A rich and uniform distribution of I compared to the original RC33 sample was seen after its exposure to HI vapor (Figs. S19–22, Supporting Information). Fig. 4f shows the decoration of CDs on graphene sheets, confirming a successful combination of CDs and RGO. The reducing time triggers an increase in iodine concentration and a decrease in both oxygen and carbon concentrations (Table 2 and Figs. S19–22, Supporting Information). The presence of iodine not only increased the electrical conductivity of RGO/CDs composite but also induced higher pseudocapacitance, thus improving its electrochemical activity [30,32,43,44].

The effect of reduction time on the porosity of RC33 sample was investigated via nitrogen physisorption and the results are shown in Fig. 5 and Table 2. The specific surface area and pore volume of the materials decreased remarkably from 337 m2 g−1 to 66 m2 g−1 and 1.17 to 0.23 cc g−1, respectively as the reduction time was varied from 0 to 30 min. These results are consistent with the SEM images illustrated in Fig. 4. Physisorption experiments (Fig. 5a) exhibit type IV isotherms with a hysteresis loop suggesting the existence of mesopores. The DFT pore size distributions (Fig. 5b) suggest that RC33 consists of micro and meso-pores with radii ranging from 0.8 to 18.0 nm while the mesopore region is narrow and centered at around 3.8 nm with prolonged HI treatment. Such micro/mesopore network is highly beneficial for ease of electrolyte penetration and fast electron transfer, which leads to enhanced electrochemical performance.

The reducing time with HI vapor treatment affects the chemical properties of the RC33. Fig. 6 a depicts the Raman spectra of the samples with two characteristic bands at ∼1340 and ∼1587 cm−1 corresponding to D from disordered/defect structure and G from graphitic carbon, respectively. The intensity ratio between D and G peaks (ID/IG) evaluates the defective structure of the carbon based materials [22,36]. The ID/IG value rose from 1.00 to a peak of 1.11 with reducing time increasing from 0 to 10 min, which might be assigned to more surface/edge defects and iodine doping [30–32,34]. However, when the RC33 was exposed to HI vapor for longer periods of time (15 and 30 min), ID/IG decreased to 1.06 and 1.01, respectively, suggesting the restoration of graphene framework caused by further reduction of aromatic iodides [30,45]. No major differences in the intensities of 2D and 2G bands (∼2690 and 2903 cm−1, respectively) were observed with prolonged reducing time. Notably, there is no Raman peak at 117 and 165 cm−1, indicating that no polyiodide formation of I3 − and I5 − on the resulting materials [32,46]. In addition, the intensities of the phenolic CO and COC stretching modes at 1563 and 1201 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra weaken with an increased reducing time (Fig. 6b) [30,34,47], strongly confirming an effective removal of oxygen functional groups on the RGO/CDs composite by HI vapor. A similar phenomenon was seen in the case of RGO after 10 min HI treatment (Figs. 23–24, Supporting Information).

The effect of HI vapor treatment on the surface chemical composition of RC33 was further evaluated by XPS. The C1s spectra of HI-RC33-10 (Fig. 7 a) have been deconvoluted into 4 peaks at 284.7, 285.5, 286.7 and 289.2 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CI, CO and OCO groups, respectively [48]. After 30 min exposure to HI vapor, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups almost disappeared whereas the CC/CC bonds became dominant with their contributions rising from 32.6% in HI-RC33-10 to 47.3% in HI-RC33-30 (Fig. 7b). Moreover, the atomic ratio of carbon to oxygen (C/O) in HI-RC33-10 was calculated to be 5.7, suggesting an efficient reduction of GO via hydrothermal process. Notably, the C/O atomic ratio further increased to 11.9 after 30 min HI acid treatment, which reveals that the exposure to HI vapor is highly beneficial for the restoration of π-conjugated graphitic carbon of RGO/CD. Fig. 7c and d illustrate the chemical states of I 3d5/2 peak in the RC33 after 10 and 30 min HI acid treatment. The I 3d5/2 peak of HI-RC33-10 consists of two components arising from neutral iodine (I2, ∼621 eV, 57.7%) and iodine anion (I−, 619 eV, 42.3%) [32]. The deconvolution of this peak in Fig. 7d suggests a conversion of iodine anion to neutral iodine with longer reducing time (100% I2 after 30 min HI treatment), indicating that aromatic iodides were further reduced by donating their electrons to restore the π conjugated system of graphene and generate neutral iodine I2 [32]. Thus, the Raman, FTIR and XPS spectra confirm the effective reduction of RGO into graphene and the successful formation of I-doped graphene/CD composites.

We investigated the electrochemical behavior of RC33 after HI treatment for variable reduction times (denoted as HI-RC33-y, y = 5, 10, 15 and 30 min) in a three-electrode configuration with 1.0 M KOH as electrolyte. The CV plots HI-RC33-y reveal similar reversible redox humps between −0.8 and −0.4 V compared to RC33, yet these humps become more pronounced in HI-RC33-10 and HI-RC33-15 samples (Fig. 8 a), which may be attributed to pseudocapacitance induced by both oxygen functional groups and I heteroatoms [3,32,43]. Besides, their GCD curves also reveal triangular and nearly symmetric shapes, suggesting a high performance supercapacitor characteristic (Fig. 8b).

The specific capacitances calculated from their CV and GCD curves (see Figs. S25 and S26, Supporting Information) are shown in Fig. 8c and d. Among HI-RC33-y samples, HI-RC33-10 has the highest specific capacitance of about 374 F g−1 at the scan rate of 2 mV s−1 compared to those of RC33 (218 F g−1), HI-RC33-5 (258 F g−1), HI-RC33-15 (311 F g−1), and HI-RC33-30 (197 F g−1). A similar tendency related to the effect of reducing time on the capacitance retention was observed. When the scan rate rises to 1000 mV s−1, the capacitance retention increases with an increased reducing time from 62.0% (0 min) to 64.6% (5 min) and reaches the highest value of 71.7% after 10 min of HI treatment, which is mainly due to enhanced electrical conductivity and ion diffusion enabled by oxygen functional groups and iodine doping [3,30,32,43,44]. However, at a higher reducing time (15 and 30 min) of HI treatment, the capacitance retention drops to 66.1 and 57.7%, respectively. This might result from the collapse of pores and reduced surface area caused by graphene restacking with prolonged reduction procedure (Table 2 and Figs. 4 and 5), which might block the ionic transportation to the electrode [3,11,42]. The improved electrochemical performance caused by iodine doping was further confirmed by the results of RGO after 10 min HI treatment (Fig. S27, Supporting Information), which delivers a maximum specific capacitance of 248 F g−1 at 2 mV s−1 and still maintains 150 F g−1 at 1000 mV s−1, retaining 60.4% of its initial capacitance. Nevertheless, these values are far lower than those of HI-RC33-10, showing the importance of the decoration of CDs on RGO for the enhanced electrochemical properties. The performance of the fabricated HI-RC33-10 is comparable to a few other doped graphene-based supercapacitors reported earlier (see Table S4, Supporting Information).

The HI-RC33-10 was further charged/discharged at a current density of 10 A g−1 (Fig. 9 a). After GCD for 10,000 cycles, the material retains 93.8% its initial capacitance, indicating an excellent stability of the HI-RC33-10 with no explicit changes observed in CV plots before and after 10,000 GCD cycles (Fig. 9b). The material still maintained a specific capacitance of 293 F g−1 at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1, which corresponds to 94.4% capacitance retention.

EIS measurements helped elucidate the impact of reduction time on the electrochemical properties of the materials. The Nyquist plots of RC33 and HI-RC-y are presented in Fig. 10 a. The semicircle intercept on the real axis at the high frequency region relates to the electrolyte resistance (Re) whereas the width of the semicircle at middle frequency region corresponds to charge transfer resistance (Rct) between ionic electrolyte and electrodes [26,36,42]. Minimal changes were observed in Re before and after HI treatment (∼4.6 Ω), indicating their similar wettability and internal resistance [5,36]. Charge transfer resistances of all HI-RC-y samples are lower than that of original RC33, implying that HI reduction enables the ionic conductivity at the interphase between electrolyte and materials.

To further explain the results from our experiments, the ion (OH−) diffusion coefficient (DOH−) was estimated:
(4) DOH− = 1/2[RT/Sn2F2Cσw]2
where R is the gas constant, T is absolute temperature, C is OH− ion concentration, F is Faraday constant, S is surface area of electrode, n is charge number of the electroactive species (n = 1 for OH−), and σw is Warburg prefactor, which is related to Re(Z) ∼ σw(2πf)−1/2 [36,42].

The diffusion coefficients calculated from the slope of the plot between Re(Z) and inverse square root of the angular frequency (Fig. 10b) are listed in Table 3 . The RC33 exhibits a lower ionic diffusion coefficient of 5.21 × 10−19 cm2 s−1 compared to HI-RC33-5 (5.62 × 10−19 cm2 s−1) and HI-RC33-10 (22.13 × 10−19 cm2 s−1), which can be ascribed to higher conductivity of the composite caused by I− doping [32,45]. Nevertheless, with prolonged reducing time, DOH- dropped to 8.80 × 10−19 cm2 s−1 and 2.52 × 10−19 cm2 s−1 after 15 and 30 min HI treatment, respectively. This may be due to the collapse of pores due to the graphene restacking with prolonged reduction procedure, which blocks the ionic transportation to the electrode, causing a decline in diffusion coefficient [3,11,42]. For comparison, the diffusion coefficient of HI-RGO-10 was also estimated to be 3.29 × 10−19 cm2 s−1, almost quadrupling that of pure RGO (Tables S3 and S5, Supporting Information). The DOH- values are consistent with the CV, GCD and EIS results, demonstrating that the improved electrochemical behavior of HI-RC33-10 is due to the faster penetration of the electrolyte to the materials.

To understand the charge storage mechanism inside the materials, we analyzed the CV sweep curves of the samples before and after HI vapor exposure to calculate the contribution of capacitive effect and diffusion controlled processes quantitatively (see Fig. S25, Supporting Information) by the following equation [36,49]:
(5)
q
∗
(
ν
)
=
q
s
∗
+
q
d
∗
=
q
(
ν − ∞
)
∗
+ c o n s t
(
ν
−
1
/
2
)
where q∗ (ν), q∗ s and q∗ d are total charge, capacitive contribution and diffusion-controlled contribution, respectively, ν is the scan rate.

From the slope of the plot between the gravimetric capacitance versus ν−1/2 (Fig. 11 a), we calculated surface capacity and diffusion controlled contributions and the values are presented in Tables S6–7. Both capacitive and pseudo-capacitive contributions rise from RC33 to HI-RC33-10, followed by a fall as the reducing time increases to 15 and 30 min. The surface capacitance of HI-RC33-10 which occupies ∼71.8% of the total capacitance at 2 mVs−1 increases to ∼98.2% at scan rate of 1000 mVs−1 (Fig. 11b), suggesting that the reaction is surface-dominant.

Based on the aforementioned experimental data, we propose a reduction mechanism of oxygen containing functional groups by means of HI vapor treatment [30,50] as illustrated in Fig. 12 . During the reduction procedure, iodine resides in the composites and exists as I− and neutral I2, which contributes to high electrical conductivity of HI-RC33-y [30,32], while both oxygen and iodine doping induces higher pseudocapacitance [11,43] and enhanced electrochemical performance. In contrast, with extended exposure to HI vapor (15 and 30 min), aromatic iodides were further reduced by donating their electrons to restore the π conjugated framework of graphene and generated neutral iodine I2, which mainly contributes towards their pseudocapacitance [30,45]. Despite its high conductivity, the severe restacking of graphene blocks the ionic transportation to the materials, causing a decline in diffusion coefficient and thus hindering the electrochemical performance. The possible redox reaction between O functional groups and I heteroatoms with OH− are as follows [11,43,44]:
(6) –C–OH + OH− ↔ –CO + H2O + e−
(7) –CO + OH− ↔ –COOH + e−
(8) –COOH + OH− ↔ –COO + H2O + e−
(9) –I + 2OH− ↔ –IO + H2O + 2e−
(10) –IO + 4OH− ↔ –IO3 + 2H2O + 4e−
(11) –I + 3H2O−↔ –IO3 + 6H+ + 6e−
(12) I2 + 2H2O ↔ –2IO− + 4H+ + 2e−
(13) IO− + 4OH− ↔ IO3 − + 2H2O + 4e−
(14) I2 + 6H2O ↔ 2IO3 − + 12H+ + 10e−

We have demonstrated a green and low-cost route for the synthesis of zucchini-derived carbon dots using waste zucchini biomass as an inexpensive and naturally available carbon precursor. The as-prepared CDs were hydrothermally treated with graphene oxide to prepare reduced graphene oxide/CD composites, followed by facile reduction by HI vapor. We found that the CDs serve as spacers to effectively hinder the restacking of graphene nanosheets, leading to higher surface area as well as total pore volume. The HI treatment has a significant effect on both the electrical conductivity and the generation of active sites for pseudocapacitance caused by oxygen/iodine containing groups, charge transfer resistance and ionic penetration in an alkaline medium, triggering changes in electrochemical behavior of the RGO/CDs. In this work, the graphene/CDs with 33% CDs and after 10 min HI treatment contains 6.1 wt.% I, possesses a surface area of 185 m2 g−1 and a total pore volume of 0.58 cc g−1. With this product, a maximum specific capacitance of 374 F g−1 was obtained at 2 mV s−1 with a superior capacitance retention of 71.7% as the scan rate increased from 2 to 1000 mV s−1. The composite also exhibited superior cycling stability over 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 with a capacitance retention of 93.8%. This research offers a sustainable procedure to fabricate supercapacitor electrode materials utilizing agricultural wastes.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

The authors thank Mr. Shengli Zhai at Nanyang Technological University, Singapore for his assistance with graphene oxide synthesis, Mr Long H. Nguyen at USyd for his assistance with freeze-drying and the ACMM (Australian Centre for Microscopy and Microanalysis) for access to the instruments for morphological measurements.
